paragraphs: Training 1 MCQ-1

1. The four main components of taxonomy (DINC) include: A. Description, Identification, Nomenclature, Classification B. Division, Identification, Naming, Categorization C. Description, Inheritance, Naming, Classification D. Documentation, Identification, Naming, Categorization Answer: A. Description, Identification, Nomenclature, Classification
2. Plant taxonomy is considered a major part of: A. Morphology B. Anatomy C. Systematics D. Ecology Answer: C. Systematics
3. Systematics includes: A. Only classification B. Only nomenclature C. Taxonomy and phylogeny D. Only identification Answer: C. Taxonomy and phylogeny

4. The fundamental aim of to systematics is: A. Name plants only B. Discover evolutionary relationships C. Study plant anatomy D. Collect herbarium specimens' Answer: B. Discover evolutionary relationships
5. Biosystematics mainly studies: A. Only morphology B. Chromosome number and hybridization C. Only anatomy D. Only ecology Answer: B. Chromosome number and hybridization
6. A dichotomous key consists of: A. Multiple access points B. Two contrasting statements C. Images only D. DNA sequences Answer: B. Two contrasting statements
7. A polyclave key is also known as: A. Single-access key B. Dichotomous key C. Multi-access key D. Floral key Answer: C. Multi-access key
8. Binomial nomenclature was introduced by: A. De Candolle B. Bentham C. Carolus Linnaeus D. Eichler Answer: C. Carolus Linnaeus
9. Phoenix dactylifera is the scientific name of: A. Mango B. Date palm C. Wheat D. Rice
10. Classification is a process of: A. Naming organisms B. Grouping and ranking organisms C. Describing organs D. Studying tissues Answer: B. Grouping and ranking organisms
11. A taxon is: A. A plant organ B. A classification rank C. A group at a given rank D. A chromosome Answer: C. A group at a given rank
12. Alpha taxonomy mainly deals with: A. Evolution B. Cytology C. Morphological description D. DNA analysis Answer: C. Morphological description
MCQ-2
1. Which of the following is a vegetative organ? A. Flower B. Fruit C. Leaf D. Seed Answer: C.
2. Which of the following is a reproductive organ? A. Root B. Stem C. Leaf D. Flower Answer: D
3. Angiosperms are characterized by: A. Naked seeds B. Seeds enclosed within a fruit C. Absence of flowers D. Cone-bearing structures only Answer: B
4. Gymnosperms are plants that: A. Have flowers and fruits B. Have seeds enclosed in ovary C. Have naked seeds D. Produce spores only Answer: C.
5. A shrub is defined as: A. Tall plant with single trunk B. Short woody plant with several stems from ground level C. Non-woody seasonal plant D. Climbing plant Answer: B
6. Malva parviflora is an example of: A. Biennial B. Perennial C. Annual D. Shrub Answer: C.
7. Dicot plants usually have: A. Fibrous root system B. Parallel venation C. Flower parts in multiples of three D. Tap root system Answer: D
8. Haustorial roots are found in: A. Mangifera indica B. Cuscuta C. Triticum aestivum D. Zea mays Answer: B
Mcq-3
1. The internode is: A. The region between two successive nodes B. The point of leaf attachment C. A storage organ D. A reproductive bud Answer: A.
2. A rhizome is: A. A vertical swollen stem B. A thickened underground stem with nodes and internodes C. A modified root D. A climbing organ Answer: B.
3. An example of a rhizome is: A. Cynodon dactylon B. Solanum tuberosum C. Allium cepa D. Opuntia ficus-indica Answer: A.
4. An example of a tuber is: A. Solanum tuberosum B. Allium cepa C. Cynodon dactylon D. Morus Answer: A.
5. An example of a bulb is: A. Allium cepa B. Solanum tuberosum C. Mentha D. Morus Answer: A.
6. Which of the following is an example of a corm? A. Cyperus rotundus B. Solanum tuberosum C. Allium cepa D. Oxalis Answer: A.
7. Subaerial stems include: A. Bulb and tuber B. Runner and stolon C. Thorn and tendril D. Rhizome and corm Answer: B.
8. A triangular stem is common in: A. Cyperaceae B. Poaceae C. Rosaceae D. Cactaceae Answer: A.
9. A quadrangular stem is found in: A. Mentha B. Triticum C. Bambusa Morus Answer: A.
10. A phylloclade is: A. A modified root B. A green flattened stem performing photosynthesis C. A climbing root D. A storage leaf Answer: B
MCQ-4.
1. A leaf without a petiole is termed: A) Petiolate B) Sessile C) Stipulate D) Imparipinnate Answer: B) Sessile
2. A flattened petiole performing photosynthesis as in Australian Acacia is called: A) Tendril B) Spine C) Phyllode D) Pulvinus Answer: C) Phyllode
3. In grasses, the phyllotaxy is: A) 1/3 B) 2/5 C) 1/2 D) 3/8 Answer: C) 1/2
4. More than three leaves arising from a node is: A) Opposite B) Radical C) Whorled D) Distichous Answer: C) Whorled
5. Leaves that fall at the end of the growing season are: A) Caducous B) Evergreen C) Deciduous D) Marcescent Answer: C) Deciduous
6. A trifoliate leaf is seen in: A) Trifolium B) Cassia C) Paris D) Fennel Answer: A) Trifolium
7. A heart-shaped leaf with deep notch at base is: A) Reniform B) Cordate C) Ovate D) Obcordate Answer: B) Cordate
8. A glabrous leaf surface means: A) Covered with woolly hairs B) Covered with glands C) Smooth, without hairs D) Covered with silky hairs Answer: C) Smooth, without hairs
MCQ-5
1. All sepals of a flower collectively form the: A. Corolla B. Perianth C. Calyx D. Androecium Answer: C
2. The pollen-bearing part of the stamen is the: A. Filament B. Ovule C. Anther D. Stigma Answer: C
3. The female reproductive part of a flower is called the: A. Stamen B. Corolla C. Pistil D. Sepal Answer: C
4. The swollen basal part of the pistil that later becomes the fruit is the: A. Style B. Stigma C. Ovary D. Filament Answer: C
5. The structure that receives pollen grains is the: A. Ovary B. Filament C. Stigma D. Sepal Answer: C
6. The collective term for calyx and corolla is: A. Corolla B. Androecium C. Perianth D. Gynoecium Answer: C
7. A flower containing both stamens and pistil is called: A. Imperfect flower B. Perfect (bisexual) flower C. Sterile flower D. Incomplete flower Answer: B
8. A flower that lacks functional reproductive organs is called: A. Imperfect flower B. Sterile flower C. Complete flower D. Bisexual flower Answer: B
9. Sepals that are free from each other are called: A. Gamosepalous B. Polysepalous C. Polypetalous D. Gamopetalous Answer: B
10. A corolla with united petals is called: A. Polypetalous B. Gamopetalous C. Polysepalous D. Apetalous Answer: B
11. A radially symmetrical flower that can be divided into equal halves by many planes is called: A. Zygomorphic B. Asymmetrical C. Actinomorphic D. Irregular Answer: C
12. The corolla type found in mustard plants is called: A. Papilionaceous B. Rosaceous C. Cruciform D. Campanulate Answer: C
13. The papilionaceous corolla is characteristic of the family: A. Brassicaceae B. Papilionaceae (Fabaceae) C. Malvaceae D. Solanaceae Answer: B

paragraphs: 1. The term "Taxonomy" was coined by: A. Carolus Linnaeus B. Theophrastus C. De Candolle D. Bentham Answer: C. De Candolle
2. The word taxonomy is derived from Greek words meaning: A. Plant classification B. Lawful arrangement C. Scientific naming D. Plant grouping Answer: B. Lawful arrangement

3. The four main components of taxonomy (DINC) include: A. Description, Identification, Nomenclature, Classification B. Division, Identification, Naming, Categorization C. Description, Inheritance, Naming, Classification D. Documentation, Identification, Naming, Categorization Answer: A. Description, Identification, Nomenclature, Classification
4. Plant taxonomy is considered a major part of: A. Morphology B. Anatomy C. Systematics D. Ecology Answer: C. Systematics
5. Systematics includes: A. Only classification B. Only nomenclature C. Taxonomy and phylogeny D. Only identification Answer: C. Taxonomy and phylogeny

6. The fundamental aim of systematics is to: A. Name plants only B. Discover evolutionary relationships C. Study plant anatomy D. Collect herbarium specimens' Answer: B. Discover evolutionary relationships
7. The term Biosystematics was introduced by: A. Linnaeus B. Turrill C. Camp and Gilly D. Mayr Answer: C. Camp and Gilly
8. Biosystematics mainly studies: A. Only morphology B. Chromosome number and hybridization C. Only anatomy D. Only ecology Answer: B. Chromosome number and hybridization
9. Description in taxonomy refers to: A. Naming a plant B. Writing features of a taxon C. Arranging taxa D. Studying evolution Answer: B. Writing features of a taxon
10. A dichotomous key consists of: A. Multiple access points B. Two contrasting statements C. Images only D. DNA sequences Answer: B. Two contrasting statements
11. A polyclave key is also known as: A. Single-access key B. Dichotomous key C. Multi-access key D. Floral key Answer: C. Multi-access key
12. The formal naming of taxa according to prescribed rules is called: A. Classification B. Identification C. Description D. Nomenclature Answer: D. Nomenclature
13. The rules of plant nomenclature are governed by the: A. ICBN B. ICNafp C. APG D. Both A and B Answer: D. Both A and B
14. Binomial nomenclature was introduced by: A. De Candolle B. Bentham C. Carolus Linnaeus D. Eichler Answer: C. Carolus Linnaeus
15. In binomial nomenclature, the first word represents the: A. Species B. Family C. Genus D. Order Answer: C. Genus
16. In writing scientific names, the genus name is written: A. In lowercase B. In uppercase C. Capitalized D. Bold only Answer: C. Capitalized
17. Phoenix dactylifera is the scientific name of: A. Mango B. Date palm C. Wheat D. Rice
18. Classification is a process of: A. Naming organisms B. Grouping and ranking organisms C. Describing organs D. Studying tissues Answer: B. Grouping and ranking organisms
19. A taxon is: A. A plant organ B. A classification rank C. A group at a given rank D. A chromosome Answer: C. A group at a given rank

20. The correct taxonomic hierarchy from higher to lower is: A. Species → Genus → Family → Order B. Kingdom → Division → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species C. Genus → Species → Family → Order D. Division → Kingdom → Class → Species Answer: B. Kingdom → Division → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species
21. Artificial classification was first established by: A. Bentham and Hooker B. Linnaeus C. Engler D. Bessey Answer: B. Linnaeus
22. Natural classification was developed by: A. Linnaeus B. Eichler C. Bentham and Hooker D. Cronquist Answer: C. Bentham and Hooker
23. The first clearly phyletic system of classification was produced by: A. Linnaeus B. Eichler C. Theophrastus D. Mayr Answer: B. Eichler
24. The APG system is based mainly on: A. Floral colour B. Leaf shape C. DNA sequence data D. Plant height Answer: C. DNA sequence data
25. Alpha taxonomy mainly deals with: A. Evolution B. Cytology C. Morphological description D. DNA analysis Answer: C. Morphological description
26. Beta taxonomy focuses on: A. Speciation B. Classification of taxa C. Molecular data D. Hybridization Answer: B. Classification of taxa
27. Gamma taxonomy studies: A. Intraspecific variation and evolution B. Naming rules C. Herbarium techniques D. Only morphology Answer: A. Intraspecific variation and evolution
28. Omega taxonomy uses data from: A. Cytology and molecular biology B. Only morphology C. Only anatomy D. Only ecology Answer: A. Cytology and molecular biology
29. The father of modern Botany is: A. Linnaeus B. Theophrastus C. Caesalpino D. De Candolle Answer: B. Theophrastus
30. The book "Species Plantarum" (1753) was published by: A. De Candolle B. Bentham C. Linnaeus D. Eichler Answer: C. Linnaeus

paragraphs: Questions (MCQs) Leaves: -3

1. Leaves arise from: A) Internodes B) Root apex C) Nodes D) Cambium Answer: C) Nodes 2. A bifacial (dorsiventral) leaf has: A) Similar upper and lower surfaces B) Different adaxial and abaxial surfaces C) Only one surface D) No petiole Answer: B) Different adaxial and abaxial surfaces 3. The upper surface of a leaf facing the stem axis is called: A) Abaxial B) Lateral C) Adaxial D) Ventral Answer: C) Adaxial 4. A leaf without a petiole is termed: A) Petiolate B) Sessile C) Stipulate D) Imparipinnate Answer: B) Sessile 5. A flattened petiole performing photosynthesis as in Australian Acacia is called: A) Tendril B) Spine C) Phyllode D) Pulvinus Answer: C) Phyllode 6. In grasses, the phyllotaxy is: A) 1/3 B) 2/5 C) 1/2 D) 3/8 Answer: C) 1/2 7. The 2/5 phyllotaxy is seen in: A) Carica papaya B) China rose C) Mimosa pudica D) Marsilea Answer: B) China rose 8. Leaves arranged in right angles at successive nodes are called: A) Superposed B) Alternate C) Decussate D) Whorled Answer: C) Decussate 9. More than three leaves arising from a node is: A) Opposite B) Radical C) Whorled D) Distichous Answer: C) Whorled 10. Leaves that fall at the end of the growing season are: A) Caducous B) Evergreen C) Deciduous D) Marcescent Answer: C) Deciduous 11. A simple leaf deeply incised almost to the midrib is: A) Pinnatifid B) Pinnatipartite C) Pinnatisect D) Palmatifid Answer: C) Pinnatisect

12. A compound leaf with terminal leaflet present is: A) Paripinnate B) Imparipinnate C) Bipinnate D) Multifoliate Answer: B) Imparipinnate 13. Bipinnate leaves are found in: A) Rosa B) Mimosa pudica C) Moringa D) Bombax Answer: B) Mimosa pudica 14. A trifoliate leaf is seen in: A) Trifolium B) Cassia C) Paris D) Fennel Answer: A) Trifolium 15. Stipules fused between two petioles are: A) Intrapetiolar B) Interpetiolar C) Free lateral D) Ochreate Answer: B) Interpetiolar 16. Needle-shaped leaves are termed: A) Linear B) Subulate C) Acicular D) Lanceolate Answer: C) Acicular 17. A heart-shaped leaf with deep notch at base is: A) Reniform B) Cordate C) Ovate D) Obcordate Answer: B) Cordate 18. A leaf margin without indentation is: A) Serrate B) Dentate C) Entire D) Crenate Answer: C) Entire 19. A leaf apex tapering gradually into a long point is: A) Acute B) Acuminate C) Obtuse D) Retuse Answer: B) Acuminate 20. A glabrous leaf surface means: A) Covered with woolly hairs B) Covered with glands C) Smooth, without hairs D) Covered with silky hairs Answer: C) Smooth, without hairs

paragraphs: MCQ - 3 stem

1. In botany, the stem is defined as: A. The underground root system of a plant B. The main axis of a plant composed of nodes and internodes C. The reproductive organ of a plant D. The photosynthetic part only
2. A node is: A. The region between two leaves B. The growing tip of the root C. The point of attachment of a leaf or twig on the stem D. A modified root
3. The internode is: A. The region between two successive nodes B. The point of leaf attachment C. A storage organ D. A reproductive bud
4. Which of the following is NOT a main function of the stem? A. Support B. Photosynthesis in all plants C. Transport of water and nutrients D. Attachment of leaves and flowers
5. The three main functions of the stem include: A. Respiration, reproduction, digestion B. Support, attachment, transport C. Protection, pollination, fertilization D. Absorption, anchorage, fixation
6. Stems are classified based on their position relative to the ground into: A. Two types B. Three types C. Four types D. Five types
7. Which of the following is an underground stem? A. Runner B. Rhizome C. Tendril D. Thorn
8. A rhizome is: A. A vertical swollen stem B. A thickened underground stem with nodes and internodes C. A modified root D. A climbing organ
9. An example of a rhizome is: A. Cynodon dactylon B. Solanum tuberosum C. Allium cepa D. Opuntia ficus-indica
10. A tuber stores food mainly in the form of: A. Protein B. Oil C. Starch D. Sugar
11. An example of a tuber is: A. Solanum tuberosum
B. Allium cepa C. Cynodon dactylon D. Morus
12. A bulb is characterized by: A. A swollen root B. A short underground stem with fleshy scale leaves C. A creeping stem D. A hollow stem Answer: B
13. An example of a bulb is: A. Allium cepa B. Solanum tuberosum C. Mentha D. Morus
14. A corm is: A. A horizontal underground stem B. A short vertical swollen underground stem C. A modified leaf D. A climbing organ
15. Which of the following is an example of a corm? A. Cyperus rotundus B. Solanum tuberosum C. Allium cepa D. Oxalis
16. Subaerial stems include: A. Bulb and tuber B. Runner and stolon C. Thorn and tendril D. Rhizome and corm
17. A runner develops from: A. Root tip B. Lower part of main axis C. Axils of lower leaves D. Terminal bud C
18. A weak plant that uses support to grow upward is called: A. Creeper B. Trailer C. Climber D. Runner
19. A triangular stem is common in: A. Cyperaceae B. Poaceae C. Rosaceae D. Cactaceae
20. A quadrangular stem is found in: A. Mentha B. Triticum C. Bambusa D. Morus
21. A hollow cylindrical stem is found in: A. Triticum B. Bambusa C. Mentha D. Nerium oleander
22. Thorns are modified: A. Roots B. Leaves C. Stems D. Flowers
23. A phylloclade is: A. A modified root B. A green flattened stem performing photosynthesis C. A climbing root D. A storage leaf
24. An example of a phylloclade is: A. Opuntia ficus-indica B. Allium cepa C. Solanum tuberosum D. Oxalis
25. Bulbils help in: A. Sexual reproduction B. Photosynthesis C. Vegetative propagation D. Transpiration

paragraphs: Plant Taxonomy - 2
1. The term taxonomy was coined by: A. Carl Linnaeus B. Charles Darwin C. Augustin Pyramus de Candolle D. John Ray
2. Taxonomy deals with: A. Study of plant physiology B. Identification, description, classification and naming of plants C. Study of plant diseases D. Study of plant ecology
3. Plant morphology is the study of: A. Internal structure of plants B. Chemical composition of plants C. External structure of plants D. Genetic structure of plants
4. Plant anatomy deals with the study of: A. External plant structure B. Internal plant structure C. Plant classification D. Plant ecology

6. Which of the following is a reproductive organ? A. Root B. Stem C. Leaf D. Flower

9. Angiosperms are characterized by: A. Naked seeds B. Seeds enclosed within a fruit C. Absence of flowers D. Cone-bearing structures only
10. Gymnosperms are plants that: A. Have flowers and fruits B. Have seeds enclosed in ovary C. Have naked seeds D. Produce spores only
11. In dioecious plants: A. Male and female flowers occur on the same plant B. Male and female flowers occur on separate plants C. Only male flowers are present D. Only female flowers are present

16. Monocot plants have: A. Two cotyledons B. Tap root system C. Parallel venation D. Reticulate venation
17. Dicot plants usually have: A. Fibrous root system B. Parallel venation C. Flower parts in multiples of three D. Tap root system
18. Fusiform root modification is seen in: A. Daucus carota B. Raphanus sativus C. Brassica rapa D. Beta vulgaris

paragraphs: 1. The term "Taxonomy" was coined by: A. Carolus Linnaeus B. Theophrastus C. De Candolle D. Bentham
2. The word taxonomy is derived from Greek words meaning: A. Plant classification B. Lawful arrangement C. Scientific naming D. Plant grouping

3. The four main components of taxonomy (DINC) include: A. Description, Identification, Nomenclature, Classification B. Division, Identification, Naming, Categorization C. Description, Inheritance, Naming, Classification D. Documentation, Identification, Naming, Categorization
4. Plant taxonomy is considered a major part of: A. Morphology B. Anatomy C. SystematicsD. Ecology
5. Systematics includes: A. Only classification B. Only nomenclature C. Taxonomy and phylogeny D. Only identification
6. The fundamental aim of systematics is to: A. Name plants only B. Discover evolutionary relationships C. Study plant anatomy D. Collect herbarium specimens
7. The term Biosystematics was introduced by: A. Linnaeus B. Turrill C. Camp and Gilly D. Mayr
8. Biosystematics mainly studies: A. Only morphology B. Chromosome number and hybridization C. Only anatomy D. Only ecology
9. Description in taxonomy refers to: A. Naming a plant B. Writing features of a taxon C. Arranging taxa D. Studying evolution
10. A dichotomous key consists of: A. Multiple access points B. Two contrasting statements C. Images only D. DNA sequences
11. A polyclave key is also known as: A. Single-access key B. Dichotomous key C. Multi-access key D. Floral key
12. The formal naming of taxa according to prescribed rules is called: A. Classification B. Identification C. Description D. Nomenclature
13. The rules of plant nomenclature are governed by the: A. ICBN B. ICNafp C. APG D. Both A and B
14. Binomial nomenclature was introduced by: A. De Candolle B. Bentham C. Carolus Linnaeus D. Eichler
15. In binomial nomenclature, the first word represents the: A. Species B. Family C. Genus D. Order
16. In writing scientific names, the genus start with: A. In lowercase B. In uppercase C. A capital letter D. Bold only
17. Phoenix dactylifera is the scientific name of: A. Mango B. Date palm C. Wheat D. Rice
18. Classification is a process of: A. Naming organisms B. Grouping and ranking organisms C. Describing organs D. Studying tissues
19. A taxon is: A. A plant organ B. A classification rank C. A group at a given rank D. A chromosome

20. The correct taxonomic hierarchy from higher to lower is: A. Species → Genus → Family → Order B. Kingdom → Division → Class → Order → Family → Genus → Species C. Genus → Species → Family → Order D. Division → Kingdom → Class → Species
21. Artificial classification was first established by: A. Bentham and Hooker B. Linnaeus C. Engler D. Bessey Answer: B. Linnaeus
22. Natural classification was developed by: A. Linnaeus B. Eichler C. Bentham and Hooker D. Cronquist
23. The first clearly phyletic system of classification was produced by: A. Linnaeus B. Eichler C. Theophrastus D. Mayr
24. The APG system is based mainly on: A. Floral color B. Leaf shape C. DNA sequence data D. Plant height
25. Alpha taxonomy mainly deals with: A. Evolution B. Cytology C. Morphological description D. DNA analysis
26. Beta taxonomy focuses on: A. Speciation B. Classification of taxa C. Molecular data D. Hybridization
27. Gamma taxonomy studies: A. Intraspecific variation and evolution B. Naming rules C. Herbarium techniques D. Only morphology
28. Omega taxonomy uses data from: A. Cytology and molecular biology B. Only morphology
C. Only anatomy D. Only ecology
29. The father of modern Botany is: A. Linnaeus B. Theophrastus C. Caesalpino D. De Candolle
30. The book "Species Plantarum" (1753) was published by: A. De Candolle B. Bentham C. Linnaeus D. Eichler
